joke.raw_joke: I cut my hand badly and went to the emergency room -- I asked the doctor if I could do my own stitches. — She looked at me and said, "suture...self".

### seed-083

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-083
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-083-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Production Duration Flag:** Target was 9.2s. Actual output is **11.31s** — 2.11 seconds over brief. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 8
- punchline_clarity: 8
- caption_readability: 8
- audio_comfort: 6
- shareability: 7

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 8
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 54/70

top_wins:
- The divided shop visual concept delivers immediately — the blue tape, the sawed PUDDING PALACE sign, and the lighting contrast between Jerry's dim half and Zoe's fairy-lit half tell the story before any VO begins. This is the hook working correctly.
- Jerry's resignation and Zoe's delight are the right emotional contrast. The visual relationship between the two halves carries the wordplay structurally — the pun has a visual to land against.
- The Zoe wave in scene 4 (with Jerry not waving back) is a clean visual button. It's the right ending.
- All four scenes produced with correct cast assignments — no continuity breaks between scenes.

critical_issues: None blocking publish. Duration overrun requires trim before final delivery.

micro_edits:
- **Duration trim (required):** Video runs 11.31s against a 9.2s brief — approximately 2.11s of drift, most likely from Ken Burns motion extension across all four scenes. Trim each scene to brief targets (Scene 01: 2.0s, Scene 02: 3.6s, Scene 03: max 0.3s, Scene 04: 3.1s). This is an edit-pass cut, not a regeneration. Target final runtime: 9.0–9.4s.
- **Scene 3 duration:** The sigh beat was specified at 0.2–0.3s maximum. If it's running longer than 0.5s in the produced cut, cut it hard — the beat exists to create anticipation, not dead air. Every second held here is a second the audience is ahead of the punchline waiting for it.
- **Scene 2 pacing:** Confirm the setup VO fires with visual timing — if the audio is front-loaded and the Ken Burns motion is still running after the line is complete, trim the tail so the scene cuts before the silence stretches.

gate_decision: CONDITIONAL_PASS
next_status: ready_to_publish
recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: 7.8

lena_final_note: The shop looks incredible. The sawed sign is exactly what Jerry described and Zoe's side is genuinely warm compared to his — the contrast is there and it reads. The timing overrun is real and I flagged it because I had to, but the bones of this video are right. Noah, I know you're going to call it correctly. Just... the visual on this one is SO good. I just needed you to know that before the scores.

noah_final_note: seed-083: CONDITIONAL_PASS. 54/70. Duration overrun is the sole blocking item — 11.31s against a 9.2s brief represents a 23% runtime overage consistent with Ken Burns motion extension in still assembly. Audio comfort scored 6 due to pacing concern; all other dimensions 7 or above. The trim is a single editing pass. Visual continuity clean across all four scenes. Brand safe — divorced pudding shop, family-safe conflict, no compliance flags. Advancing to ready_to_publish pending trim confirmation. Lena's read on the visual quality is accurate.

---

### seed-089

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-089
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-089-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Critical Production Duration Flag:** Target was 8.8s. Actual output is **6.04s** — 2.76 seconds under brief. This is a 31% compression. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 4
- punchline_clarity: 7
- caption_readability: 7
- audio_comfort: 5
- shareability: 6

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 7
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 45/70

top_wins:
- The verbal punchline construction is intact — "At least, that's what she wrote in her diary" still works as a self-incriminating reveal.
- Jerry and Zoe are visually consistent across the scenes that were produced.

critical_issues:
- **Cold open compressed to non-functional duration.** Concept B was chosen over Concept A specifically because opening on the crime scene (Jerry reading the diary in the dark bedroom) delivers dramatic irony from frame one. At 6.04s total for a four-scene video with 5.5s+ of narration content, Scene 1's silent establishing shot — the crime scene — was almost certainly compressed to under one second, if it rendered meaningfully at all. Scene 1 was budgeted at 2.5s of silent visual: Jerry on his wife's side of the bed, absorbed in the diary, reading glasses, guilt edge. This visual IS the concept. Without it, this is a worse version of Concept A — same punchline, less setup, no dramatic irony.
- **Pacing failure:** At 6.04s, even the audio delivery will feel rushed. The 0.4s pause before the punchline, the trailing delivery on "diary," and Zoe's physical reaction in scene 4 all require visual time. A 6.04s runtime cannot accommodate four scenes of meaningful duration.

micro_edits: N/A — not fixable in a single editing pass. The production timing is structurally wrong. This requires Vince to reassemble with correct scene durations.

gate_decision: FAIL
next_status: queued_for_scene_production

**Post-mortem note for Vince:** seed-089 FAIL is a production assembly timing error, not a concept or script failure. The joke is strong. Concept B is the correct choice. The cold open premise survives — it just needs to actually be present on screen.

Return instructions:
- Scene 01 (crime scene): must run **minimum 2.0s**, ideally 2.5s. Silent. No VO. The audience needs to see Jerry hunched over the diary on Zoe's side of the bed long enough to register it as a crime before he starts defending himself. This is the entire value proposition of Concept B.
- Scene 02 (hallway defense setup): 2.7s with VO for the setup line.
- Scene 03 (beat): 0.3–0.4s maximum.
- Scene 04 (punchline + Zoe reaction): 3.0–3.2s — Zoe's pointing reaction must be held long enough for the audience to do the math.
- Target total: 8.5–9.0s.

recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: N/A — not advancing in this state. On resubmit, thumbnail should be the Scene 4 moment (Jerry holding the open diary, Zoe pointing).

lena_final_note: I'm not going to pretend this one is okay, because it's not. The cold open was the whole reason Jerry picked Concept B over Concept A. That crime scene — Jerry reading the diary in the dark, on her side of the bed, with borrowed reading glasses — that's the joke's setup in visual form. At 6.04 seconds, it's gone. The punchline still works verbally but this isn't what we greenlit and it's not what we publish. Noah... I trust your call. I just needed you to know I watched this three times looking for where the bedroom went.

noah_final_note: seed-089: FAIL. 45/70. Hook strength scored 4 — a critical dimension below threshold. Audio comfort scored 5 — below threshold. Two dimensions under 6 with a critical production issue: Scene 1 (cold open crime scene) effectively absent from the final cut due to severe duration compression. 6.04s actual against an 8.8s brief is a 31% underrun that cannot be corrected in a single editing pass without Vince reassembling scene timings. The concept is sound. The execution is not. Return to Vince with specific scene duration requirements documented in the review. Lena's identification of the missing cold open is correct. That is the critical failure point.

---

### seed-084

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Patient / Narrator | Vince Clip — Handyman persona (adapted: Practical Guy Who Cut His Hand) | Vince is the practical cynic who would cut his hand doing something reasonable, go to the ER without drama, and recount the whole thing in a flat "this is what happened" register. His "process realist" archetype fits the setup delivery perfectly — he's not embarrassed, just explaining. |
| Doctor (female) | Zoe Sparks — Young Doctor persona | This is literally Zoe's persona. She delivers "suture self" with breezy professional helpfulness, completely unaware that it's a pun, as if she says it to everyone and it's just the obvious advice. The contrast between her cheerful competence and Vince's deadpan processing is the visual comedy. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Vince: No camera equipment. Plain casualwear. One hand wrapped in a bandage or holding gauze — the injury is visual context, not just stated. Slight wince if the scene requires a reaction, but his expression is fundamentally unbothered.
- Zoe: White coat, stethoscope, clipboard — standard Young Doctor presentation. Her usual orange-magenta hair and teal glasses are visible under/with the coat. She's professional and warm. She does not know "suture self" is funny.
- Shot framing: ER exam room or curtained bay. Vertical composition works well with Vince seated on an exam table, Zoe standing — height difference creates natural visual balance in 9:16.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Vince to camera, one hand visible with bandage, explaining the situation with mild exasperation. He asked a reasonable question. He is reporting the outcome.
- **Punchline:** Zoe delivers her line to Vince (not to camera), clipboard in hand, expression bright and confident. Hold on Vince's face — the moment he processes what she said. His expression should shift from neutral to "did she just..." without fully committing to a reaction. The audience does the math.

---

### seed-084

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-084
concepts_count: 2
Milo script: MISSING from payload (using raw joke). Working script: Setup: "I cut my hand and asked the ER doctor if I could do my own stitches." Punchline: "She said, 'Suture self.'"
Riley casting honored: Vince Clip (Handyman) as patient. Zoe Sparks (Young Doctor) as doctor. ER exam room setting.

---

**Concept A — "The Exam Room"**
Style: ER curtained bay, two-person scene, Zoe delivers the pun without knowing it's a pun
Duration: 9.0s — Needs room for the two-person exchange and Vince's processing beat.

Master video prompt:
"Hospital ER exam bay, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Vince Clip (spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, goatee, plain dark t-shirt, one hand wrapped in white gauze/bandage) seated on a blue exam table with paper sheet. Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, white doctor's coat, stethoscope, clipboard) standing beside him. Curtained bay, medical supply counter, overhead fluorescent lighting. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The ER
A hospital emergency room exam bay — a blue vinyl exam table with crinkled paper, a thin curtain partially drawn on one side, a counter with medical supplies (gauze rolls, tape, a small suture kit tray with curved needle and thread visible). Overhead fluorescent lighting, cool blue-white tones. Vince Clip sits on the exam table — a man with wild spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, goatee, wearing a plain dark t-shirt. His right hand is wrapped in a white gauze bandage with a small spot of red showing through. He holds the wrapped hand in his lap with his uninjured left hand. His expression is flat, unbothered — this is an inconvenience, not a crisis. Standing beside the exam table, Zoe Sparks — a young woman with orange-magenta two-tone hair, round teal glasses, wearing a white doctor's coat over her teal jacket, a stethoscope around her neck. She holds a clipboard and pen, reviewing something with professional concentration.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.2s): SETUP — The Question
Same ER exam bay. Vince Clip on the exam table (spiky black hair, glasses, goatee, dark t-shirt, bandaged hand) faces the viewer. His expression is mild exasperation — he's recounting what happened. One hand (the injured, bandaged one) rests in his lap; the other makes a small explanatory gesture. He asked a reasonable question. He is reporting the outcome. Beside him, Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, white coat, stethoscope, clipboard) stands with professional composure, pen poised over her clipboard. The suture kit tray on the counter behind them is visible — curved needle, thread, small scissors. The fluorescent light buzzes overhead. Vince is the kind of man who would ask if he could do his own stitches.
Caption: "I cut my hand and asked the ER doctor if I could do my own stitches."

Scene 3 (5.2–5.6s): THE BEAT
Same exam bay, framing on both characters. Zoe Sparks (white coat, teal glasses, clipboard) looks up from her clipboard. She has an answer. Her expression shifts to bright, helpful confidence — the face of a doctor with an obvious solution. She makes eye contact with Vince on the exam table. She is about to say the most helpful and most devastating thing.

Scene 4 (5.6–9.0s): PUNCHLINE — The Advice
Same ER exam bay. Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, white coat, stethoscope) faces Vince on the exam table, clipboard lowered to her side. Her expression is warm, helpful, slightly proud of herself — she has just given excellent medical advice. She does not know "suture self" sounds like "suit yourself." She says it to every patient. It is the correct term. On the exam table, Vince Clip (spiky black hair, glasses, goatee, bandaged hand) has frozen. His mouth is slightly open. His eyes behind his rectangular glasses have narrowed — not in pain but in processing. The faintest twitch at the corner of his mouth — he heard it. He is deciding whether she knows what she said. She does not. The suture kit tray is visible on the counter between them. The fluorescent light hums. Vince's expression is the precise midpoint between "did she just..." and choosing not to ask.
Caption: "She said, 'Suture self.'"

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–5.2s setup | 5.6–9.0s punchline
Voice: Vince flat, matter-of-fact for setup. 0.4s pause. Punchline is Zoe's voice — bright, professional, zero irony. Or: Vince narrates the whole thing including quoting her, in which case his delivery of "suture self" is flat quotation.
Risk: LOW. Standard ER set, two characters. "Suture" may confuse some viewers — the caption helps. Fallback: remove the ER set, Vince tells the story to camera against a plain background, Zoe not shown.
Jerry hook: "Two-person scene with perfect dynamics — Zoe's oblivious helpfulness vs. Vince's slow-dawning 'did she just.' The suture kit visible on the counter ties the visual and verbal pun together."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 9.0s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 Zoe delivery + Vince processing, Scene 2 setup + bandaged hand], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.2s]

---

**Concept B — "The Waiting Room After"**
Style: Vince in a waiting room chair post-stitches, recounting the story to camera, stitched hand visible
Duration: 8.4s — Tighter solo delivery, punchline is the quote.

Master video prompt:
"Hospital waiting room, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Vince Clip (spiky black hair, glasses, goatee, dark t-shirt) slumped in a plastic waiting room chair. His right hand now has visible stitches — a neat row of black suture thread across his palm. He holds the stitched hand up slightly, looking at it, then at the viewer. Fluorescent lighting, blue plastic chairs, a health poster on the wall. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Aftermath
A hospital waiting room — a row of blue plastic chairs against a pale wall, a health awareness poster (something generic about hand-washing), a small side table with outdated magazines. Fluorescent overhead lighting, cool tones. Vince Clip sits in one of the chairs — spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, goatee, dark t-shirt. He's slumped slightly, the posture of a man who has been here too long. His right hand rests palm-up in his lap — a neat row of black suture stitches crosses his palm, the wound freshly closed. A small adhesive bandage strip sits next to the stitches. He looks at the viewer with the flat expression of someone who has a story.

Scene 2 (2.0–4.8s): SETUP — The Story
Same waiting room. Vince Clip in the chair (glasses, goatee, dark t-shirt) faces the viewer. He lifts his stitched hand slightly — displaying the evidence. His expression is mild exasperation, recounting. His other hand makes a small "so I asked" gesture. The stitches on his palm are visible — neat, professional, clearly done by someone competent. He asked if he could do them himself. He did not get to do them himself. The waiting room is quiet around him. A clock on the wall shows it's been a while.
Caption: "I cut my hand and asked the ER doctor if I could do my own stitches."

Scene 3 (4.8–5.1s): THE BEAT
Same chair. Vince looks at his stitched hand. Then back at the viewer. His expression shifts to the faintest shadow of amusement — or possibly annoyance. It's hard to tell with Vince.

Scene 4 (5.1–8.4s): PUNCHLINE — The Quote
Same waiting room. Vince Clip in the chair (glasses, goatee, stitched hand) looks at the viewer. He delivers the quote flatly — he is repeating what someone said to him. His expression is the Vince special: complete deadpan with something unresolved behind the eyes. He heard the pun. He processed the pun. He chose not to acknowledge the pun. He is telling you about it so you can decide. His stitched hand is still visible in his lap — the evidence that she did, in fact, do them herself. He did not suture himself. She sutured him. The pun was free.
Caption: "She said, 'Suture self.'"

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–4.8s setup | 5.1–8.4s punchline
Voice: Vince flat throughout. 0.3s pause. Punchline delivered as a quote — slightly different register, still flat. No reaction after.
Risk: LOW. Single character, simple set. Fallback: plain background, no waiting room.
Jerry hook: "The visible stitches are the proof that the pun happened in real life. Vince's refusal to react to it is funnier than any reaction would be. Cheapest concept, might be the funniest."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 8.4s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline + stitched hand, Scene 2 setup + hand display], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.1s]

---

quality_gate: PASS
status: ready_for_jerry_review
